gate_type: wp_ajax authentication cookie + capability check (view_woocommerce_reports or export_woocommerce_orders)
reasoning: The wp_ajax_order_exporter hook is registered as logged-in-only (wp_ajax_, not wp_ajax_nopriv_), so unauthenticated attackers cannot reach ajax_gate() at all. Inside ajax_gate(), the global handler branch (lines 530-538) dispatches to WC_Order_Export_Ajax::ajax_export_download_bulk_file() with no nonce check when $_REQUEST['tab'] is absent — the check_admin_referer() at line 546 is only reachable via the tab-routed branch. The gate (authentication cookie + view_woocommerce_reports or export_woocommerce_orders capability) cannot be bypassed by the attacker; however, in a CSRF attack the gate is satisfied by the victim's own browser session, not something the attacker must guess or steal. CSRF does not require the attacker to obtain the gate value — it requires the victim to already hold it. WooCommerce shop managers routinely hold these capabilities. Therefore: gate_breachable=true (victim's session satisfies the gate), severity stays MEDIUM. The nonce value (woe_nonce) is generated via wp_create_nonce() and embedded only in authenticated admin pages, making it unguessable by the attacker — but because the vulnerable branch (ajax_export_download_bulk_file) has NO nonce check at all (phpcs:ignore NonceVerification comment at line 68-69), the absence of a nonce to guess is exactly the vulnerability. An attacker crafts a cross-origin POST to wp-admin/admin-ajax.php?action=order_exporter&method=export_download_bulk_file (no tab parameter) and the victim's browser sends the session cookie, triggering a full WooCommerce order export with attacker-controlled export_bulk_profile parameter.
